claims.claim_text: The opening dateline presents a specific Seattle incident on November 27, 2025 at 3:17 AM.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Consider a late-night retail launch during a major shopping event.**
claims.rationale: This is a concrete real-world dateline with no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The anecdote names a specific person and gives precise alert counts within two minutes.
claims.anchor_text: Sarah Martinez was dreaming about her upcoming vacation when her phone started buzzing. Three alerts, then ten, then forty-seven in the span of two minutes.
claims.suggested_rewrite: An on-call engineer's phone started buzzing as alert volume surged immediately after launch.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed war story with named-entity and precision details that cannot be verified.

claims.claim_text: The anecdote gives specific latency, error-rate, and Kubernetes-version details for the outage.
claims.anchor_text: Half-asleep, she logged into their observability platform to find a wall of red. User-facing latency for the chat interface had spiked from 800ms to 12 seconds. Error rates had jumped from 0.1% to 42%. The underlying Kubernetes clusters (running v1.35) were perfectly healthy. CPU was low. Memory was stable. The problem wasn't their infrastructure—it was their consumption of managed Cloud AI services.
claims.suggested_rewrite: She found a wall of red: latency and error rates had surged even though the underlying cluster health looked normal, pointing to managed AI service consumption rather than node saturation.
claims.rationale: These are fake-precise outage specifics inside an unattributed anecdote.

claims.claim_text: The anecdote gives a specific diagnosis timeline, quota failure mechanism, revenue impact, and fix timing.
claims.anchor_text: Four hours later, after escalating to their cloud provider's enterprise support, they found the cascading failure. The team had relied on a global endpoint for their foundation models without realizing the regional rate limits. A sudden spike in complex user queries exhausted their on-demand token quota. Retries amplified the traffic, triggering API throttling, which caused upstream services to queue requests until memory exhausted. The total revenue impact? $2.3 million in abandoned shopping carts. The fix? Purchasing provisioned throughput and implementing cross-region inference profiles—a change that took minutes to apply but hours to diagnose.
claims.suggested_rewrite: After escalating and investigating, the team traced the failure to exhausted model-service quota and retry amplification, then stabilized the system by moving to reserved capacity and better multi-region routing.
claims.rationale: This uses highly specific business-impact and timeline details inside an unsourced war story.

claims.claim_text: Amazon Bedrock's current model catalog includes Claude 4.6 entries, DeepSeek V3.x entries, and Meta Llama 4 models.
claims.rationale: The Bedrock models page directly lists Claude Opus 4.6, Claude Sonnet 4.6, DeepSeek V3.1/V3.2, and Llama 4 models.

claims.claim_text: Amazon Bedrock documents model feature support including Agents, Knowledge base query, Model evaluation, and Provisioned Throughput.
claims.rationale: Bedrock's feature matrix explicitly includes these feature columns.

claims.claim_text: Bedrock offers geographic and global cross-Region inference profiles that route requests across AWS Regions to absorb bursts, and on-demand requests can be constrained by quotas or peak usage.
claims.rationale: The cross-Region inference doc states these profile types, routing behavior, and quota-related burst handling.

claims.claim_text: The module states that Bedrock cross-region inference profiles necessarily introduce variable latency.
claims.anchor_text: Cross-region inference profiles dynamically route requests across multiple AWS regions to absorb spikes, but they introduce variable latency that your upstream microservices must be configured to handle gracefully.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Cross-region inference profiles dynamically route requests across multiple AWS regions to absorb spikes, so you should validate the resulting latency and compliance behavior for your application.
claims.rationale: The AWS doc supports multi-region routing and throughput benefits, but not this exact latency assertion.

claims.claim_text: For custom models in Bedrock, Provisioned Throughput provides dedicated compute capacity and returns a provisioned model ARN to use for inference.
claims.rationale: The Provisioned Throughput custom-model doc explicitly states dedicated compute capacity, guaranteed throughput, and provisioned model ARN usage.

claims.claim_text: The module says Bedrock Provisioned Throughput guarantees capacity regardless of noisy neighbors.
claims.anchor_text: Once purchased, custom model inference uses dedicated model IDs and ARNs, guaranteeing your capacity regardless of noisy neighbors.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Once purchased, custom model inference uses dedicated model IDs and ARNs, allocating dedicated throughput capacity for the provisioned model.
claims.rationale: The AWS doc supports dedicated capacity and guaranteed throughput, but not this specific noisy-neighbor framing.

claims.claim_text: OCI Generative AI is a fully managed service that supports chat, embeddings, rerank, OpenAI-compatible APIs, and access to pretrained models through the Console, API, or CLI.
claims.rationale: OCI's overview page directly lists these tasks and access methods.

claims.claim_text: OCI Generative AI is available across commercial (OC1), government (OC4), and sovereign (OC19) region families.
claims.rationale: The OCI regions page explicitly lists OC1, OC4, and OC19 sections.

claims.claim_text: OCI Generative AI's model-by-region tables distinguish on-demand, dedicated-only, and interconnect-only availability.
claims.rationale: The model-by-region legend explicitly defines these availability markers.

claims.claim_text: OCI dedicated AI clusters are used to fine-tune custom models or host pretrained, custom, and imported models, and they are not shared with other tenancies.
claims.rationale: The dedicated AI clusters doc directly states both the workloads they support and the non-shared tenancy model.

claims.claim_text: The module says enterprise scale requires OCI custom model import, fine-tuning, and dedicated AI clusters.
claims.anchor_text: While OCI allows the use of pretrained hosted models via console, CLI, and API, enterprise scale requires custom model import, fine-tuning, and hosting on **dedicated AI clusters**.
claims.suggested_rewrite: While OCI allows the use of pretrained hosted models via console, CLI, and API, teams that need dedicated hosting or custom-model workflows use **dedicated AI clusters** for import, fine-tuning, and serving.
claims.rationale: The OCI docs support these capabilities but not the absolute claim that enterprise-scale deployments require them.

claims.claim_text: OCI enterprise agent features include an OpenAI-compatible Responses API, MCP tool support, vector stores, memory features, and NL2SQL support.
claims.rationale: OCI's overview explicitly lists the Responses API, MCP, vector stores, memory, and SQL Search (NL2SQL).

claims.claim_text: Foundry Models sold directly by Azure include all Azure OpenAI models and selected models from other providers billed through Azure with Microsoft's SLA and support.
claims.rationale: The Azure page states these billing, support, and catalog-scope details directly.

claims.claim_text: Azure's sold-direct catalog currently includes the GPT-5.4 series models gpt-5.4, gpt-5.4-pro, gpt-5.4-mini, and gpt-5.4-nano.
claims.rationale: The GPT-5.4 section on the Azure page lists these specific model IDs.

claims.claim_text: Azure Foundry documents global standard and global provisioned deployments that dynamically route traffic across Azure datacenters, with provisioned throughput offering reserved capacity and lower latency variance.
claims.rationale: The deployment-types page directly describes global routing and provisioned throughput behavior.

claims.claim_text: Standard Vertex AI operations do not have a general global location; users choose supported regions for datasets, models, and other regional resources.
claims.rationale: The Vertex AI locations doc explicitly says Vertex AI doesn't support a global location.

claims.claim_text: Generative AI on Vertex AI supports a global endpoint at locations/global, but global endpoints do not guarantee data residency or in-region ML processing and do not support tuning plus some batch prediction workflows.
claims.rationale: The Vertex generative AI deployments page states the global endpoint behavior and limitations directly.

claims.claim_text: Vertex AI's global endpoint tables currently list Gemini 2.5 and Gemini 3.1 preview model IDs.
claims.rationale: The global endpoint section explicitly lists Gemini 2.5 and Gemini 3.1 preview entries.

claims.claim_text: Classic Foundry Agent Service docs mark classic agents as deprecated, set retirement for March 31, 2027, limit hub-based projects to older models, and require registration for GPT-5 family models.
claims.rationale: The classic Agent Service model-region page contains all of these details on the same page.

claims.claim_text: OCI Generative AI Enterprise AI Agents reached General Availability on March 31, 2026.
claims.rationale: The OCI release note gives the GA date explicitly.

claims.claim_text: OCI Generative AI added API keys on January 21, 2026 and on-demand AI guardrails on February 9, 2026.
claims.rationale: Both dated release-note entries appear on Oracle's Generative AI release notes page.

claims.claim_text: The module attributes 23% versus 46-60% slack and ML-based resource prediction to Borg since 2013.
claims.anchor_text: 4. **Historical Efficiency:** Google's Borg system (predecessor to Kubernetes) has used ML for resource prediction since 2013, achieving just 23% resource slack compared to 46-60% slack for manually-managed jobs.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. **Historical Efficiency:** Google has published autoscaling work showing that ML-based resource prediction can reduce resource slack compared with manual provisioning.
claims.rationale: The cited numbers belong to later Google Autopilot work, not Borg-as-written, so the sentence mixes system, date, and metric claims inaccurately.

claims.claim_text: The module uses a specific 500-alert example for alert fatigue outside a code block.
claims.anchor_text: A sudden spike in API latency might trigger 500 alerts across different microservices.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A sudden spike in API latency can trigger a flood of alerts across different microservices.
claims.rationale: This is an illustrative statistic presented as fact, and the precise number is not attributable.

claims.claim_text: The asymmetric-cost section uses specific dollar and session-loss figures as facts.
claims.anchor_text: Wasting $100 on an unused node is annoying. Losing 10,000 user sessions to timeout errors is disastrous.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Wasting money on unused capacity is annoying. Losing a large number of user sessions to timeout errors is disastrous.
claims.rationale: These are uncited illustrative quantities that should not remain as precise numbers.

claims.claim_text: The Common Mistakes table says Vertex locations/global omits batch tuning and provides no data residency guarantees.
claims.span_hint: table: Common Mistakes / Using Global Endpoints for Tuning
claims.anchor_text: Read the docs: `locations/global` omits capabilities like batch tuning and provides no data residency guarantees. Choose a specific region.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Read the docs: `locations/global` doesn't guarantee data residency and doesn't support capabilities such as tuning and some batch prediction workflows. Choose a specific region.
claims.rationale: The underlying limitation is real, but the exact 'batch tuning' wording does not match Google's documented limitations.

claims.claim_text: The Common Mistakes table says OCI introduced dedicated API keys for generative models in early 2026.
claims.span_hint: table: Common Mistakes / Ignoring API Key Security
claims.rationale: Oracle's release notes show API key support arriving on January 21, 2026.

claims.claim_text: The Common Mistakes table says Bedrock Provisioned Throughput for custom models uses dedicated model IDs or ARNs to provide guaranteed capacity.
claims.span_hint: table: Common Mistakes / On-Demand for Production
claims.rationale: The Bedrock custom-model Provisioned Throughput doc directly covers dedicated capacity and provisioned model identifiers.

further_reading.title: Amazon Bedrock Cross-Region Inference
further_reading.why_relevant: Best primary source for Bedrock geography, global routing, burst handling, and Provisioned Throughput tradeoffs.

further_reading.title: Microsoft Foundry Deployment Types
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains Azure global, data-zone, and regional deployment behavior, including routing, data processing location, and latency tradeoffs.

further_reading.title: Vertex AI Deployments and Endpoints
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers the global endpoint, data residency caveats, supported models, and feature limitations that drive architecture decisions.

further_reading.title: OCI Generative AI Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Summarizes OCI model access, dedicated AI clusters, Responses API, tools, memory, and NL2SQL in one place.

notes: No shared citation pool was found for ai-ml-engineering/ai-infrastructure under docs/citation-pools on 2026-04-21, so supported claims use claim-level proposed_url values.
